title: Pair<T> with operator== and operator!=
prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Pair<T>` template class so it supports `operator==` and `operator!=` for comparing two pairs of the same type.

## Input
- Line 1: two integers A1 and A2 (the first pair)
- Line 2: two integers B1 and B2 (the second pair)

## Output
- Line 1: `equal: 1` if the pairs are equal, `equal: 0` otherwise
- Line 2: `not_equal: 1` if the pairs are not equal, `not_equal: 0` otherwise

## Requirements
1. `Pair<T>` has member variables `m_first` and `m_second` of type `T`.
2. Constructor takes two `T` arguments.
3. `bool operator==(const Pair& rhs) const` returns true if both members match.
4. `bool operator!=(const Pair& rhs) const` returns the negation of `operator==`.
5. The sample test uses two identical pairs (both should be equal).
6. Hidden tests include cases where pairs differ.

## Notes
- Only `<iostream>` is needed.

title: Template findMax — Complete the Function
prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `findMax` function template so it returns the largest element in an array of any type that defines `operator>`. The exercise combines function templates with operator overloading by also overloading `operator>` for a `Score` struct.

## Input
- Line 1: integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10) — number of integers
- Line 2: N integers

## Output
- Print exactly one line: `max: <value>` where `<value>` is the largest integer.

## Requirements
1. `template <typename T> T findMax(const T* arr, int size)` — iterates through the array and returns the largest element using `operator>`.
2. The function must work for any `T` that defines `operator>`.
3. `main` reads N integers into an `int` array, calls `findMax`, and prints the result.
4. Hidden tests include N=1 and the maximum at different positions.

## Notes
- Only `<iostream>` is needed. Do not use `std::max` or `std::max_element`.
